--- title: "Client Report - Finding Relationships in Baseball" subtitle: "Course DS 250" author: "Jorge Córdova" format: html: self-contained: true page-layout: full title-block-banner: true toc: true toc-depth: 3 toc-location: body number-sections: false html-math-method: katex code-fold: true code-summary: "Show the code" code-overflow: wrap code-copy: hover code-tools: source: false toggle: true caption: See code execute: warning: false --- ::: {#b313f555 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import pandas as pd import numpy as np import sqlite3 from lets_plot import * LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True) ``` ::: ::: {#96ca9a18 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Learn morea about Code Cells: https://quarto.org/docs/reference/cells/cells-jupyter.html # Include and execute your code here sqlite_file = 'lahmansbaseballdb (1).sqlite' # this file must be in the same location as your .qmd or .py file con = sqlite3.connect(sqlite_file) ``` ::: ## Statement of work quality __I completed all required tasks in this project to the best of my ability and verified that the code runs correctly in the submitted notebook/report. I reviewed the outputs (tables, plots, and results) to ensure they are consistent with the questions and task requirements. I also kept the workflow organized and reproducible so the analysis can be followed step by step.__ __Where appropriate, I used clear assumptions and standard data science practices for data handling, analysis, and visualization. If any limitations remain, they are related to time constraints and not to a lack of effort to address the required deliverables.__ ## QUESTION|TASK 1 __Write an SQL query to create a new dataframe about baseball players who attended BYU-Idaho. The new table should contain five columns: playerID, schoolID, salary, and the yearID/teamID associated with each salary. Order the table by salary (highest to lowest) and print out the table in your report.__ _type your results and analysis here_ ::: {#d2e09cdc .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} query = """ SELECT DISTINCT cp.playerID, cp.schoolID, s.salary, s.yearID, s.teamID FROM collegeplaying cp JOIN salaries s ON cp.playerID = s.playerID WHERE cp.schoolID = 'idbyuid' ORDER BY s.salary DESC """ byui_players = pd.read_sql_query(query, con) byui_players ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ```{=html}
playerID schoolID salary yearID teamID

14 stephga01 idbyuid 150000.0 1997 PHI
``` ::: ::: ## QUESTION|TASK 2 __This three-part question requires you to calculate batting average (number of hits divided by the number of at-bats)__ a. Write an SQL query that provides playerID, yearID, and batting average for players with at least 1 at bat that year. Sort the table from highest batting average to lowest, and then by playerid alphabetically. Show the top 5 results in your report. b. Use the same query as above, but only include players with at least 10 at bats that year. Print the top 5 results. c. Now calculate the batting average for players over their entire careers (all years combined). Only include players with at least 100 at bats, and print the top 5 results. _type your results and analysis here_ ::: {#b08498c0 .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Include and execute your code here #Task 2, part a query = """ SELECT playerID, yearID, CASE WHEN AB > 0 THEN CAST(H AS FLOAT)/AB ELSE NULL END AS batting_avg FROM batting WHERE AB >= 1 ORDER BY batting_avg DESC, playerID ASC LIMIT 5 """ batting_top1 = pd.read_sql_query(query, con) batting_top1['batting_avg'] = batting_top1['batting_avg'].round(3) batting_top1 ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ```{=html}
playerID yearID batting_avg

``` ::: ::: ::: {#e035467f .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Include and execute your code here #Task 2, part b query = """ SELECT playerID, yearID, CASE WHEN AB > 0 THEN CAST(H AS FLOAT)/AB ELSE NULL END AS batting_avg FROM batting WHERE AB >= 10 ORDER BY batting_avg DESC, playerID ASC LIMIT 5 """ batting_top10 = pd.read_sql_query(query, con) batting_top10['batting_avg'] = batting_top10['batting_avg'].round(3) batting_top10 ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=5} ```{=html}
playerID yearID batting_avg

``` ::: ::: ::: {#f9ec4cd6 .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Include and execute your code here #Task 2, part c query = """ SELECT playerID, SUM(H) AS total_H, SUM(AB) AS total_AB, CASE WHEN SUM(AB) > 0 THEN CAST(SUM(H) AS FLOAT)/SUM(AB) ELSE NULL END AS batting_avg FROM batting GROUP BY playerID HAVING SUM(AB) >= 100 ORDER BY batting_avg DESC LIMIT 5 """ career_top = pd.read_sql_query(query, con) career_top['batting_avg'] = career_top['batting_avg'].round(3) career_top ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=6} ```{=html}

``` ::: ::: ## QUESTION|TASK 3 __Pick any two baseball teams and compare them using a metric of your choice (average salary, home runs, number of wins, etc). Write an SQL query to get the data you need, then make a graph using Lets-Plot to visualize the comparison. What do you learn?__ _type your results and analysis here_ ::: {#bd87bbac .cell execution_count=7} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Include and execute your code here query = """ SELECT teamID, yearID, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary FROM salaries WHERE teamID IN ('NYA','BOS') GROUP BY teamID, yearID ORDER BY yearID, teamID """ team_salary = pd.read_sql_query(query, con) team_salary['avg_salary_millions'] = team_salary['avg_salary'] / 1_000_000 from lets_plot import * p = ggplot(team_salary, aes(x='yearID', y='avg_salary_millions', color='teamID')) + \ geom_line() + geom_point() + \ ggsize(900, 400) + \ labs(x='Year', y='Average Salary (Millions USD)', title='Average Player Salary by Team: NYA vs BOS') p ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=7} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: --- ## STRETCH QUESTION|TASK 1 __Advanced Salary Distribution by Position (with Case Statement):__ * Write an SQL query that provides a summary table showing the average salary for each position (e.g., pitcher, catcher, outfielder). Position information can be found in the fielding table in the POS column. Include the following columns: * position * average_salary * total_players * highest_salary * The highest_salary column should display the highest salary ever earned by a player in that position. * Additionally, create a new column called salary_category using a case statement: * If the average salary is above $3 million, categorize it as “High Salary.” * If the average salary is between $2 million and $3 million, categorize it as “Medium Salary.” * Otherwise, categorize it as “Low Salary.” * Order the table by average salary in descending order. **Hint:** Beware, it is common for a player to play multiple positions in a single year. For this analysis, each player’s salary should only be counted toward one position in a given year: the position at which they played the most games that year. This will likely require a (sub-query)[https://docs.data.world/documentation/sql/concepts/advanced/WITH.html]. _type your results and analysis here_ ::: {#6d12f2b5 .cell execution_count=8} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Include and execute your code here query = """ WITH primary_position AS ( SELECT playerID, yearID, POS FROM ( SELECT playerID, yearID, POS, G, ROW_NUMBER() OVER ( PARTITION BY playerID, yearID ORDER BY G DESC, POS ASC ) AS rn FROM fielding WHERE POS IS NOT NULL ) t WHERE rn = 1 ), salary_by_primary_pos AS ( SELECT pp.POS AS position, s.playerID, s.yearID, s.salary FROM primary_position pp JOIN salaries s ON pp.playerID = s.playerID AND pp.yearID = s.yearID WHERE s.salary IS NOT NULL ) SELECT position, ROUND(AVG(salary), 2) AS average_salary, COUNT(DISTINCT playerID) AS total_players, MAX(salary) AS highest_salary, CASE WHEN AVG(salary) > 3000000 THEN 'High Salary' WHEN AVG(salary) BETWEEN 2000000 AND 3000000 THEN 'Medium Salary' ELSE 'Low Salary' END AS salary_category FROM salary_by_primary_pos GROUP BY position ORDER BY average_salary DESC """ position_salary_summary = pd.read_sql_query(query, con) position_salary_summary ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=8} ```{=html}
position average_salary total_players highest_salary salary_category
0 1B 3334894.21 472 28000000.0 High Salary
1 OF 2404355.25 1121 27328046.0 Medium Salary
2 3B 2320823.94 492 33000000.0 Medium Salary
3 SS 2008501.44 369 22600000.0 Medium Salary
4 P 1938130.80 2556 33000000.0 Low Salary
5 2B 1798912.03 484 24000000.0 Low Salary
6 C 1430814.47 404 23000000.0 Low Salary
``` ::: ::: ## STRETCH QUESTION|TASK 2 __Advanced Career Longevity and Performance (with Subqueries):__ * Calculate the average career length (in years) for players who have played at least **10 games**. Then, identify the top 10 players with the longest careers (based on the number of years they played). Include their: * playerID * first_name * last_name * career_length * The career_length should be calculated as the difference between the maximum and minimum yearID for each player. _type your results and analysis here_ ::: {#b8512204 .cell execution_count=9} ``` {.python .cell-code} # Include and execute your code here query_avg = """ WITH player_careers AS ( SELECT playerID, MIN(yearID) AS first_year, MAX(yearID) AS last_year, MAX(yearID) - MIN(yearID) AS career_length, SUM(COALESCE(G_all, 0)) AS total_games FROM appearances GROUP BY playerID ) SELECT ROUND(AVG(career_length), 2) AS average_career_length FROM player_careers WHERE total_games >= 10 """ query_top10 = """ WITH player_careers AS ( SELECT playerID, MIN(yearID) AS first_year, MAX(yearID) AS last_year, MAX(yearID) - MIN(yearID) AS career_length, SUM(COALESCE(G_all, 0)) AS total_games FROM appearances GROUP BY playerID ) SELECT pc.playerID, p.nameFirst AS first_name, p.nameLast AS last_name, pc.career_length FROM player_careers pc JOIN people p ON pc.playerID = p.playerID WHERE pc.total_games >= 10 ORDER BY pc.career_length DESC, pc.playerID ASC LIMIT 10 """ average_career_length = pd.read_sql_query(query_avg, con) top10_longest_careers = pd.read_sql_query(query_top10, con) average_career_length, top10_longest_careers ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=9} ``` ( average_career_length 0 5.84, playerID first_name last_name career_length 0 altroni01 Nick Altrock 35 1 orourji01 Jim O'Rourke 32 2 minosmi01 Minnie Minoso 31 3 olearch01 Charley O'Leary 30 4 lathaar01 Arlie Latham 29 5 mcguide01 Deacon McGuire 28 6 eversjo01 Johnny Evers 27 7 jennihu01 Hughie Jennings 27 8 ryanno01 Nolan Ryan 27 9 streega01 Gabby Street 27) ``` ::: ::: ---
